title: CLAUDE.md
text: socialchange R package decomposing social change components intraindividual change population turnover. package implements multiple decomposition methods analyzing aggregate-level outcomes change time due : - Intraindividual/within-unit changes (e.g., people changing attitudes) - Population turnover (cohort replacement, mortality, migration, coming--age) - Event-driven changes (e.g., countries joining/leaving EU)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package two main intellectual motivations: Skepticism APC models: package partly critique Age-Period-Cohort (APC) methods. APC models suffer identification problem (age = period − cohort) require untestable assumptions resolve . package offers decomposition-based alternatives directly interpretable. (critique developed future vignettes/papers.) Improving Firebaugh’s CR-IC decomposition: Firebaugh’s algebraic linear CR-IC decompositions classical approach separating cohort replacement intracohort change, known limitations (treatment entering/exiting cohorts, inability handle non-linearities, residuals linear method). package improves methods via model-based counterfactuals (AD+, Model methods cr_ic()) , fundamentally, via simulation-based individual-level decomposition (sim_social_change(), decompose_aggregated()), tracks demographic events directly rather inferring algebraically.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package implements three main decomposition approaches: decompose_events(): Decomposes change units enter/exit population discrete time points Works two datasets: events data (entry/exit dates) outcomes data (values time point) Formula interface: Outcome ~ Unit + Time Separates change “change” (within-unit) “replacement” (turnover) components Computes counterfactuals showing happen change replacement decompose_aggregated(): data aggregated age covariates Uses microsimulation approach randomly order demographic events Requires: stacked panel data age, period, cell counts Requires: fun_y function computes outcome data Decomposes : intraindividual change, coming--age, mortality, (optionally) migration sim_social_change(): Forward simulation social change dynamics User provides functions : outcome (fun_y), mortality, coming--age, migration, state transitions Returns detailed event--event records showing exact contribution component

title: CLAUDE.md
text: CR-IC decomposition (R/cr_ic.R): Classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change decomposition using algebraic, linear, model-based approaches APC models (R/apc.R): Age-Period-Cohort models using orthogonal polynomial contrasts handle identification issues

title: CLAUDE.md
text: three main approaches use data.table extensively: - Input data data frames data.tables - Formula interfaces follow R conventions: Outcome ~ Unit + Time - Functions internally rename columns standardized names (unit, index, outcome, etc.) - Time/period columns coerced numeric calculations - functions return S3 objects class attributes custom print/plot methods

title: CLAUDE.md
text: decompose_aggregated() properly handle within-cell transitions (e.g., smokers becoming non-smokers). function decomposes change : - Intraindividual change - Mortality - Coming--age - Migration (optional) input data includes transitions cells, transition effects separately identified instead get absorbed intraindividual change component. Additionally: Mortality coming--age estimated simple population differences periods: mortality = n1 - n2 coming_of_age = n2 - n1 transitions occur, differences longer accurately reflect demographic events code uses pmax(0, ...) prevent negative mortality/coming--age counts, masks underlying problem Cells net inflow transitions show 0 mortality even deaths occurred Workaround: fundamental limitation aggregated decomposition approach. simulation function sim_social_change() properly tracks transitions separate component, decompose_aggregated() recover already-aggregated data. practice: Use decompose_aggregated() data without significant within-cell transitions, aware transition effects misattributed intraindividual change.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: now feed simulated data event-based decomposition: event-based decomposition exactly recovered results.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] }  library("socialchange") simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) # we know that y depends only on age here model <- lm(y ~ age, data = stacked_data) predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decompresult <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decompresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.3758 #>  At end (modeled)          0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1208 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1292 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

20) * 1 / 20)] }  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.37580 #>  At end                    0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12491 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12491 #>    - Mortality            -0.06031 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06460 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) model_no_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_no_gender)) #> [1] 0.6870648 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_no_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.37580 #>  At end (modeled)          0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - title: Decomposing General Social Survey data: Attitudes on Homosexuality
text: cr_ic() function implements four methods separating intracohort change (IC) cohort replacement (CR): algebraic decomposition (AD), linear decomposition (LD), two model-based improvements (AD+ Model). detailed explanation method, including replication Firebaugh (1989), see Replications: Firebaugh vignette.  APC analysis gss_homosex dataset, see APC vignette.

title: Simulating social change
text: Building simulation requires three steps: Define population Define outcome function variable interest Define population dynamics (coming age, mortality, - -migration) Step 1: first step define initial population. example, define simple population aged 20 39, uniform distribution age 30, declining age 39. dataset define needs least two columns: age n. can add columns want describe complicated populations, ’ll stick simple setup now. ’ll call row dataset population cell. extreme case, can also define population individuals n just 1. ’s simple population: Step 2: Next, define variable interest want simulate. , define function takes arguments population cells time continuous variable. Time really needs continuous - need know outcome every individual population terms discrete ‘years’ (whatever time scale), every point -well. example, use outcome dependent age: age 20, outcome 0, age 40 outcome 1, values linearly increasing age. purely function age, time, real-world survey, outcome “support investing longevity research” “support increasing pensions” - ’ll just use latter example: defined population terms one variable (age), members age also identical outcome value. Step 3: next step define population dynamics. requires defining four functions: fun_mortality(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_outmigration(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_coming_of_age(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n fun_inmigration(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n functions defined period--period. practice, often years. instance, mortality function return counts define many members population die within next period, population cell. -migration function defined way, just number people migrating population cell. coming age function -migration functions different: return data frame describes new population members added period--period. functions flexible enough describe complicated real-world population dynamics. first simple example, define functions way keeps population stable: don’t migration, mortality coming--age balances perfectly - every period 100 new members age 20 added, 100 members age 30 die. four functions optional, just define coming--age mortality function: ’re now ready start simulation: simulation result displays overview period, well summary total decomposition change. results year show indeed population stable: end every period population 1550, stable mean 0.38 - .e, 38% population supports increasing pensions. However, stability bit deceptive, everyone’s opinion actually keeps constantly changing. can see looking “Intraindividual change” component, +0.25. Roughly speaking, without population turnover, expected mean support increasing pensions increased quite bit, 63%. makes sense, ’ve designed simulation way support increases age. turn, younger people much less likely support increasing pensions. Hence, enter population, effect decreasing mean. true mortality. Hence, population turnover component negative. simulation, two components balance exactly, leading expected complete stabilty year--year mean. slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change (women ), components half . previous scenario, overall outcome stable, intraindividual change well population turnover, cancelling . can also construct case shows intraindividual change, making outcome function dependent time (possibly covariates, ), age. expected, results show outcome increased 0.1 year--year, change due intraindividual change: Conversely, can also construct scenario change explained population turnover - older cohorts replaced younger cohorts. , define outcome function individual’s birth cohort: constants chosen two cases align. important thing outcome depends birth cohort (.e. cohort = year - age). results show exact dynamic – initial mean 0.3 increase 0.1 every year –, different explanations: Now change completely explained population turnover. one ever changes mind, social change occurs older cohorts low outcome values die younger cohorts higher outcome values follow. Hence, mortality coming--age contribute equally total change.

what: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
text: socialchange package provides methods decomposing aggregate-level social change components intraindividual change population turnover. Population turnover includes replacement individuals (mortality coming--age) migration. package implements event-based, aggregated-data, simulation-based decomposition approaches, well classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change (CR-IC) decompositions Age-Period-Cohort (APC) models.

EU formed 1952, countries entered exited 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020. what: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Decomposes total change outcome two periods cohort replacement (CR) intracohort change (IC) components. Four methods available:

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
code: cr_ic(data, formula, weight = NULL, model = NULL)

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: data data frame one row per respondent (one row per cohort-period cell `weight` supplied). formula formula form `outcome ~ period + cohort`. weight Name weight variable string. `NULL`, observations weighted equally. model Either model formula (e.g., `~ period * cohort`) fitted model object supports `predict()`. formula supplied fitted weighted `lm`. Required AD+ Model methods.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: list class `cr_ic_decomposition` components: `summary` `data.table` summarising IC CR full period       method. `detailed` `data.table` year--year results       consecutive pair periods. `periods` Numeric vector periods found data. `cohort` `data.table` cohort-level IC CR contributions       (`model` supplied, otherwise `NULL`). `model` fitted supplied model object, `NULL`.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: - **AD** (algebraic decomposition): Decomposes spanning cohorts IC   CR exactly. Entering exiting cohorts split assigned   entirely CR, Firebaugh notes "problematic". - **LD** (linear decomposition): Fits linear model `y ~ period + cohort`   uses coefficients attribute change. Handles entering/exiting   cohorts capture non-linearities produces residual. - **AD+**: Extends AD imputing missing outcome values entering/exiting   cohorts model supplied via `model`, applies algebraic   decomposition. Produces residual; quality depends model fit. - **Model**: Like AD+ replaces ** cohort-specific values (just   missing ones) model predictions. Acts form regularization,   can help small cohorts noisy estimates. AD LD always computed. AD+ Model require `model` argument.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Firebaugh, G. (1992). social change come ? Estimating relative contributions intra-cohort change population turnover. *Population Research Policy Review*, 11(1), 61–74.
